title: How a Computer Program Called Lean Is Changing Math Forever
summary: What if a computer could check your math homework and guarantee every answer is 100% correct? That’s kind of what a program called Lean does—but for super-advanced math proofs that can be hundreds of pages long! A new book, The Proof in the Code, tells the story of how Lean went from a tool for fixing software bugs to a game-changer for mathematicians. Lean was created in 2013 by a software engineer named Leo de Moura. At first, it was meant to make sure computer code had no mistakes. But a few math professors saw its potential: if Lean could check code logic, why not check math logic? Both coding and math proofs are built step-by-step, and one tiny error can mess everything up. Getting Lean to understand math wasn’t easy. Mathematicians had to translate basic ideas—like “2 is not equal to 1”—into code, and build huge libraries of definitions. It took years of teamwork from people all over the world. By 2025, tens of thousands of users were using Lean. It even helped train an AI called AlphaProof, which scored a silver medal at the 2024 International Mathematical Olympiad! Lean can’t come up with new proofs on its own, but it can verify them faster than any human. That means mathematicians can trust their results and build on them more quickly. As the book shows, Lean is becoming a “truth machine” that makes math more reliable than ever. Who knows? Maybe one day it’ll help solve the biggest math puzzles out there.
why_it_matters: With Lean, the math you learn in school might one day help create rock-solid proofs for safer airplanes, secure computers, and maybe even solve puzzles no human could check alone.

keywords.explanation: A computer program that can check if a mathematical proof is correct, step by step.
A careful, step-by-step argument that shows a math statement is absolutely true.
The set of rules that tell us if a chain of reasoning makes sense and has no mistakes.
Big collections of pre-written facts and definitions that Lean can use to check new ideas.
Mistakes or errors in computer code that make a program do something wrong.
An artificial intelligence that learned to solve hard math problems using Lean and won a silver medal.

options: A computer program that checks math
A planet in our solar system
questions.correct_answer: A computer program that checks math

questions.question: What competition did AlphaProof do well in?
What can Lean NOT do by itself?
What did mathematicians have to do to get Lean to understand math?
About how many people were using Lean by 2025?

background_read: For centuries, mathematicians wrote proofs on paper, but sometimes small mistakes were missed for years. In recent decades, computers have been used to check proofs, but it was very difficult.
Lean started as a tool for fixing software bugs but grew into a 'proof assistant' that can verify every single logical step. Now mathematicians around the world build libraries of definitions for it.

AlphaProof is an AI that learned to compete in the International Mathematical Olympiad (a world championship math contest for top high school students) by using Lean, and it won a silver medal in 2024.
Article_Structure: WHO: Software engineer Leo de Moura created Lean; mathematicians worldwide helped build it, along with the AI AlphaProof.
WHAT: Lean checks long, complex math proofs perfectly, catching errors no human could see. It also trained an AI to solve olympiad problems.
WHERE: Used online by people everywhere.
WHY: To make mathematics fully reliable and speed up new discoveries by building a trustable foundation.

perspectives.description: Mathematicians celebrate that Lean makes their work perfectly trustworthy, letting them build safely on top of earlier results.
Some worry that relying on computers might take the fun out of math, because coming up with fresh new ideas still needs human imagination.
